observations.recommendation:
We recommended and reiterated that Management:
Strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances in accordance with the provisions provided under COA Circular No. 97-002;
Issue demand letters to officials and employees, who have outstanding cash advances as of December 31, 2021, especially those who are still in the active service, copy of which to be submitted to the Auditor with proof of service and dated to enforce settlement of unliquidated cash advances; and
File request for authority to write-off dormant or long outstanding unliquidated cash advances, if necessary, to the Office of the Auditor, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team copies of the Memorandum of Agreement and supporting documents on the subject fund transfers and ensure their submission for any subsequent fund transfers; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain SL per project/activity of the source agency.

We recommended that Management:
Submit the required lacking supporting documents in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445;
Submit explanation/justification on the deficiencies noted; and
Ensure that the procurement of medicines and medical supplies conforms with existing government laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer immediately submit the subject CY 2021 DVs with complete supporting documents, collections accounts and necessary monthly reports including the lacking DVs for the current year and prior years. Otherwise, the subject disbursements will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of financial reports. Also, Management must institute appropriate remedial measures to ensure that the required financial reports are submitted promptly without the need to demand from time to time.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the guidelines in the preparation and use of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for non-cash transactions, preparation of cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement journal, general journal with recapitulation and posted to GLs and SLs prescribed under the MNGAS for LGUs to come up with a more detailed and reliable financial records and FS.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly adhere to the aforecited provisions on the procurement of infrastructure projects.  Henceforth, all perfected contracts together with their supporting documents must be submitted for review and evaluation to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from execution of contract;
Direct concerned officials to submit lacking supporting documents and ensure that procurement for infrastructure projects is properly supported with complete documentation; and
Submit explanation/justifications on the deficiencies noted, otherwise, impose liquidated damages for completing construction works beyond the stipulated date of the contract.

We recommended that Management
Ensure that all the changes in the PPAs are incorporated in a supplemental or revised Annual Investment Plan and that development projects to be funded out of the 20 percent DF are well-planned and attainable within the desired time frame in order to maximize allocated funds to have an immediate impact on the socio-economic development of the LGU; and
Strictly adhere to the provisions of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 in formulating PPAs in order to achieve the maximum benefits for its constituents.

We recommended that Management prioritize utilization of the remaining BGCM funds to fully implement the identified PPAs during the State of Calamity. If remained unutilized during the period of State of Calamity until September 12, 2022, unless lifted earlier or further extended, remaining funds should be returned to the National Treasury.

We recommended that Management,
a. Require the Inventory Committee to continuously make utmost effort to
conduct a complete and detailed physical inventory of all the LGU’s
properties in accordance with the new guidelines and procedures
prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31 2020, prepare
and submit, within the deadlines set, the RPCPPE together with the Report
on the Physical Count of the Road Network System of the LGU;
b. Execute a deed of donation to recipient schools of buildings, equipment
and other PPE donated by the LGU and to draw a JEV dropping off the
respective PPE accounts in the LGU’s books of accounts and transmit it to
the respective divisions or autonomous schools together with other
documents relative to the buildings and other PPE items to enable their
Accountant or Bookkeeper to record it in their books of accounts;
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify to the proper Inventory
account semi-expendable items with estimated useful life of more than one
year but less than the capitalization threshold and recorded as PPE. These
items must be recorded as expense upon their issuance and tender of
corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip to the end-user for proper
monitoring, control and accountability; and
d. Require the GSO and Municipal Accountant to make a final reconciliation
of their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and
adjust the books accordingly, in order to come up with a reliable PPE
balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all depreciable
assets are provided with depreciation and draw a journal entry voucher to take up
unrecorded current and prior years’ depreciation expense to presently fairly the
financial statements in accordance with IPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer
exert efforts to reconcile the reciprocal Due to LGUs and Due from LGUs accounts
between the Municipality and the Province and Barangays.
A journal entry voucher must be prepared to take up in the books of the
Municipality any adjustments noted during the reconciliation process.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant prepare the Journal Entry
Voucher to record the reclassification/adjustment of accounts in the books. The
suggested Journal Entry is shown below:
Account Name Debit Credit
Due to Officers and Employees ₱ xxx
Accounts Payable ₱ xxx
Due to LGUs Xxx

wear and tear;

Require the Inventory Committee to completely
fill-up the information on the whereabouts and
conditions of all properties physically counted
to determine other unserviceable PPE items. The
Inventory Committee must recommend them for
disposal while the properties still carry their
respective salvage value and submit a written
report to the GSO of the result of the actual
physical inventory conducted so the latter
could undertake appropriate and feasible
remedies for any deficiencies noted;

Require the GSO to file an application for
disposal through the Local Chief Executive with
the Office of the Audit Team Leader, by
conducting first an appraisal of the properties
for disposal in accordance with the Manual on
Appraisal of Government Properties except Real
Properties and Works of Art; and

Any losses of government property determined
during the physical count must be reported and
accountability thereof must be determined.
Future losses of properties by any accountable
officer or person in custody must be properly
and immediately disseminated to concerned
offices. The appropriate request for relief
from accountability must be requested from the
Commission on Audit within the period
prescribed by existing rules and regulations,
together with the supporting documentary
requirements, which would include the basic
notice of loss, report stating the facts and
circumstances of the loss, valuations and other
necessary details or documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer see to it that the Petty Cash fund is fully settled at the end of every year and its unutilized balance is refunded/returned to the municipal treasury and deposited to the AGDB. Strict adherence to Section 48 of the Manual on the LGU-NGAS, particularly the full liquidation of the Petty Cash account at the end of the year, is enjoined.

We recommended that Management exert effort to require these formerly elected officials to liquidate/refund/settle their CAs immediately and to stop granting elected officials CAs other than for their official traveling expenses.

We recommended that Management establish an effective and efficient internal control on supply and property management, specifically on the: a) centralization of custody, handling and issuance of supplies and materials in the GSO; b) preparation and regular submission of the SSMI to the Municipal Accountant for the timely recognition of expenses; and c) complete conduct of the required physical count of all supplies and properties. We further recommended securing certifications from recipients of previously booked supplies and materials that no longer exist per physical count to serve as basis for the dropping of the non-existent balances from the books of accounts.

We recommended the continuous and sustained effort of the Inventory Committee in conducting an annual physical inventory of the properties of the Municipality and submit the same within the prescribed period. We also suggest that all information required in the RPCPPE be properly filled up to ascertain its accuracy and completeness.
We further recommended that Management require the General Services Office and the Accounting Office to prioritize the reconciliation process and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace the period or date of acquisition of the subject Land Improvement, Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts and compute the depreciation expense based on the estimated useful life of assets as provided under COA Circular No. 2003-007. Prior years’ depreciation must be taken up as Prior Years Adjustment to be closed to Government Equity and current year’s depreciation must be recorded as expense.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to properly account all the land properties of the Municipality by gathering relevant supporting documents from the Municipal Assessor’s Office and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to arrive at a more reliable Land account balance in the Financial Statements in accordance with PPSAS 17 and other applicable accounting standards; and
Require the Assessor’s Office to reconcile their records with the GSO and Accounting Office. Also, ensure that the municipal lot for public plaza located at Brgy. Poblacion East valued at ₱427,000 per RPCPPE and FS is properly recorded and make necessary actions to establish absolute ownership of the same.

We recommended that Management continue its commitment to faithfully remit trust collections, however, the current administration still has to exert effort in reconciling and establishing the unremitted trust collections from prior years’ transactions and once established, accordingly remit the same to the respective government agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort to identify the sources of the difference between the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts through reconciliation and effect immediately the adjustments upon verification. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant must also determine if the purposes for which the transfers between funds are made have already been achieved or completed, hence must be liquidated and recorded in the separate books of accounts to present fairly the balances of the said accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to formulate a three-year program for strategic allocation of the SEF and obtain/secure the required DepEd approved SIP and DEDP and other information from the DepEd RO on the allocation of each school from the National Budget as contained in the NEP, which supposedly were the basis for the preparation and approval by the LSB of the SEF Budget and furnish the Audit Team, for reference and review purposes. Henceforth, the LSB must strictly follow the mandated required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the LCPC to:
Actively perform their duties and responsibilities to ensure full strengthening and implementation of the LCPC PPAs; and
Widen the scope of the PPAs for LCPC as enumerated in Section II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 and prioritize the PPAs with long term effects/involvement on children.

We recommended that Management require the municipal officials and employees concerned to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of perfected infrastructure contracts and its supporting documents to COA for a timely and complete conduct of its auditorial and technical review.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) post the Notices of Awards and Notices to Proceed on the procurements of goods and infrastructure projects in the PhilGEPS website in compliance with the provisions of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to:
Explain/justify the noted inconsistencies and lapses on the bid opening, preliminary examination of bids and post-qualification process of the two infrastructure projects and submit the needed documents for verification;
Explain why the project Concreting of Pathway, Construction of Plant Box and Construction of School Perimeter Fence was awarded and implemented despite the inconsistency of dates of the eligibility documents of the bidders;
Submit the lacking documents in order to support the validity and propriety of the expenditures; and
Carefully examine all the documents submitted by the bidders to determine whether they comply with and are responsive to all requirements and conditions as specified in the Bidding Documents.
We further recommended that Management require the members of the BAC/ (Technical Working Group (TWG) and the Office of the Municipal Accountant to constantly scrutinize the validity, legality, correctness and completeness of the supporting documents in all the procurement process and/or all claims against the government funds to prevent the issuance of Notice of Suspensions/Disallowances.
These recommendations are without prejudice to the suspension or disallowance of the payments made for the procurement of the subject two infrastructure projects due to the foregoing deficiencies noted and incomplete supporting documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Supply Officer to conduct pre-repair evaluation/inspection and post repair evaluation/inspection of all its government vehicles subjected to repairs; and
Require that the Certificate of Acceptance and RWM be prepared by the Supply Officer and approved by the Local Chief Executive.

We recommended that the Accounting Office immediately submit the subject disbursement vouchers and their supporting documents for further evaluation. Management must strictly adhere to the policies and guidelines on rendering overtime services set forth in CSC and DBM JC No. 1, s. 2015 and the documentary requirements for its payment prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officers and employees to immediately submit the lacking supporting documents pertaining to the above travel claims for further evaluation. Also, Management must refrain from using government funds for unnecessary group travel and ensure the completeness of supporting documents for those travels allowed by existing rules and regulations.

We reiterated, with amendments, the following recommendations to Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer, the MPDO and the Municipal Accountant to submit the list of all on-going projects and those that are to be implemented during the year including the Quarterly Reports on Publicized Government PPAs which are already due for submission; and
In the succeeding year’s preparation of the said reports and the deadlines set in COA Circular No. 2013-004 must be strictly observed.
We recommended the utilization of the LDRRMF for PPAs that would mitigate and reduce the impact and/or effect of calamities and adversities that may befall the Municipality.

We recommended that Management require the Chairperson of GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and other concerned officials to implement all the projects and activities programmed in the GAD Plan and Budget to ensure the optimum utilization of the GAD Funds for the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD focal person to:
Strictly comply with the existing rules and regulations prescribing the guidelines in the formulation of Annual GPB, development of GAD activities and implementation and monitoring of the GPB; and
Utilize the GAD Fund only for acceptable PPAs that would address the identified gender issues in the locality based on the RA 9710 Magna Carta for Women, PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and other relevant international commitments.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD focal person to submit a copy of the Annual GPB and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report to COA within the prescribed period for the timely audit so that recommended corrective measures may also be issued in the appropriate time.

We recommended that Management establish and maintain GAD database to serve as basis in the identification of priority gender issues that are crucial for the Municipality’s evidence-based planning and policy making.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all department heads involved in development projects to ensure that funds allocated thereon are utilized for projects identified as priority socio-economic and environmental development in accordance with the guidelines/policies prescribed under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended Management’s adherence to the
accounting and reporting guidelines for the
Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund
(LDRRMF) set forth in COA Circular No.
2012-002, with emphasis on the following:

The Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) must see to
it that in the preparation of the budget
proposal for the ensuing year, the approved
LDRRMFIP must be the basis in making the
current year appropriations for QRF, MF-MOOE
and MF-CO.

The unexpended balance of the MF-CO for the
previous year must be forwarded and made as
continuing appropriation, retained as to amount
and specific program or project. When asked as
to the basis of said continuing appropriation,
the previous LDRRMFIP/s must be presented
showing the portion of projects for capital
outlays. While the report/data on the
unexpended balance of QRF and MF-MOOE for the
current year must be forwarded to the
Accounting Office at the end of the year for
appropriate action to transfer said amount to
the Special Trust Fund; 

The Municipal Accountant, in drawing the
disbursement voucher to effect the transfer,
must properly validate and verify the
correctness of the reported unexpended balance
by the MBO to ensure that there would be no
excess or insufficiency of fund transfer. The
unexpended balances of QRF and MF-MOOE at year
end, lodged in the Special Trust Fund and
specifically segregated into the year it was
obtained, must be reported and communicated to
the LDRRMO for the preparation of the
Investment Plan. Further, it is advised that
correcting entries be effected on Trust
Liabilities-DRRM for the amount of
understatement in the account;

The LDRRMO must prepare LDRRMFIP annually
composed of the plan for the current year to be
forwarded to the MBO and for the previous
years’ unexpended balance of QRF and MF-MOOE
based on the report received from the
Accounting Office. The LDRRMFIP must include
under a separate caption, the list of projects
and activities charged to the unexpended DRRMF
of previous years after proper evaluation of
the need to use the Special Trust Fund. Also,
ensure that the 30 percent QRF is provided in
the LDRRMFIP as a lump-sum appropriation to
allow its immediate use in times of calamity
and disaster; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant  to
determine,  once  and  for  all,  the  collectability  of  these  Receivable
accounts together with the dormant cash advances and  to  document  the
request for write-off from the Commission  on  Audit  the  account  balances
which   remained   non-moving   for   10   years   or   more    and    where
settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained in accordance  with
the guidelines and procedure provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005  dated
December 19, 2016 for fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that  the  Municipal  Government  endeavor  to  further
improve its process in recording and overall management of  RPT  Receivables
through the following strategies:

1. Require the Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer  and  Municipal
   Accountant to strictly follow the proper process  in  recording  of
   RPT Receivables as prescribed under Section 248 of RA No. 7160  and
   Section 20, Volume I of New Government Accounting System for  Local
   Government Units


2. Require Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal  Accountant  a
   duly signed certified list showing the name of  taxpayers  and  the
   amount  due  and  collectible  every  beginning  of  the  year  for
   recording purposes;

3. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to  draw  a  JEV  to  record  the
   RPT/SET Receivables based on the certified list from the Office  of
   the Municipal Treasurer and ensure that  the  recorded  receivables
   are based on actual reference documents for  fair  presentation  of
   the Financial Statements and in adherence to Section 20, Vol. I  of
   the LGU-NGAS Manual; and

4. Cause the posting of a Notice of Delinquency in the Payment of Real
   Property Tax at the main entrance of  the  Municipal  Hall  and  in
   other publicly accessible  place  and  conspicuous  place  in  each
   barangay of the municipality in accordance with Section 254  of  RA
   No. 7160.

We recommended and reiterated that Management:


a. Once again, convene the Inventory  Committee,  conduct  the  annual
   physical count of all its property, plant and equipment and  submit
   the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant  and  Equipment
   to the Office of the Auditor;

b. Require the General Services Officer, in  the  performance  of  his
   mandated functions, to give preferential attention  in  maintaining
   an updated Property Cards per category on all property, plant,  and
   equipment as recorded in the list of Property, Plant, and Equipment
   of accounting records, and thereafter as  the  transaction  occurs.
   She must also facilitate the proper conduct of the  physical  count
   and preparation of the Report in the prescribed form;


c. Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain property ledger  cards
   consistent with their accounting records;


d. Require both offices to reconcile property  records  ensuring  that
   the balances shown in the prepared RPCPPE agreed  with  the  supply
   and property records  and  the  book  balances  in  the  accounting
   records, and vice versa; and


e. For any  resulting  difference,  the  Accounting  and  the  General
   Services Office must work out on  tracing  the  reconciling  items,
   establish their existence/non-existence and  determine  from  there
   any further action that may be undertaken.

We recommended that the Management require:


a. The General Services Office (i) to  secure  insurance  of  all  its
   insurable property from the GSIS  in  order  to  mitigate  possible
   losses on the part of the  Municipality;  and  (ii)  to  submit  an
   inventory list of insurable properties; and


b. The Budget Officer to ensure that appropriate amount is included in
   the budget to cover for the property insurance premiums.

We recommended that Management:


1. Provide for a bigger storage room wherein unserviceable  properties
   as well as assets  still  use  for  operations  could  be  properly
   secured and safeguarded against the elements of wear and tear;


2.  Require  the  Inventory  Committee  to  completely   fill-up   the
   information on the whereabouts and  conditions  of  all  properties
   physically counted to determine other unserviceable PPE items.  The
   Inventory Committee must recommend  them  for  disposal  while  the
   properties still carry their respective salvage value and submit  a
   written report to the GSO of the  result  of  the  actual  physical
   inventory conducted so the latter could undertake  appropriate  and
   feasible remedies for any deficiencies noted;


3. Require the GSO to file an application  for  disposal  through  the
   Local Chief Executive with the Office of the Audit Team Leader,  by
   conducting first an appraisal of the  properties  for  disposal  in
   accordance with the Manual on Appraisal  of  Government  Properties
   except Real Properties and Works of Art; and


4. Any losses of government property determined  during  the physical
   count  must  be  reported  and  accountability  thereof   must   be
   determined. Future losses of properties by any accountable  officer
   or person in custody must be properly and immediately  disseminated
   to concerned offices.  The  appropriate  request  for  relief  from
   accountability must be  requested  from  the  Commission  on  Audit
   within the period prescribed by  existing  rules  and  regulations,
   together with the supporting documentary requirements, which  would
   include the basic notice of loss,  report  stating  the  facts  and
   circumstances of the loss, valuations and other  necessary  details
   or documents.

We recommended that:


a. The Municipal Planning Officer and the Budget Officer  ensure  that
   proposed  PPAs   chargeable   against   the   Fund   are   properly
   identified/itemized and the related information  such  as  specific
   timetable and expected output are defined in the AIP to  facilitate
   monitoring and evaluation;


b. The Municipal Planning and Development Officer abide strictly  with
   the guidelines in the formulation of the 20 percent Development
   Plan based on equitable  allocation  of  funds  pursuant  to  Joint
   Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 of the DILG and DBM;


c. Implement immediately and on timely basis the  identified  projects
   under the 20 percent DF;


d. The members of the BAC schedule early  all  procurement  activities
   pertaining to development projects to pave  way  for  their  timely
   implementation and completion; and


e. Refrain from utilizing the  developmental  fund  for  non-allowable
   programs/projects.  The utilized amounts for non-allowable projects
   must be restituted  to  the  fund  in  order  not  to  deprive  the
   stakeholders of the benefits they may  derived  from  developmental
   projects in terms of socio-economic development  and  environmental
   management.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the  LDRRMF
based on the approved AIP to support  disaster  risk  management  activities
following the policies and procedures  on  the  utilization  of  the  LDRRMF
under paragraph 6.0 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated  March  25,
2013.

We also recommended that the Management instruct the LDRRMO to  submit
the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of  LDRRMF  on  or  before
the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to 5.1.5 of  COA  Circular
No. 2012-02, to facilitate timely verification and audit thereof.

We recommended Management’s adherence to the accounting and  reporting
guidelines for the Local Disaster Risk Reduction  Management  Fund  (LDRRMF)
set forth in COA Circular No. 2012-002, with emphasis on the following:


1. The Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) must  see  to  it  that  in  the
   preparation of the  budget  proposal  for  the  ensuing  year,  the
   approved LDRRMFIP must be the basis  in  making  the  current  year
   appropriations for QRF, MF-MOOE and MF-CO. The  unexpended  balance
   of the MF-CO for the previous year must be forwarded  and  made  as
   continuing  appropriation,  retained  as  to  amount  and  specific
   program or project. When asked as to the basis of  said  continuing
   appropriation, the previous LDRRMFIP/s must  be  presented  showing
   the portion of projects for capital outlays. While the  report/data
   on the unexpended balance of QRF and MF-MOOE for the  current  year
   must be forwarded to the Accounting Office at the end of  the  year
   for appropriate action to transfer said amount to the Special Trust
   Fund;


2. The Municipal Accountant, in drawing the  disbursement  voucher  to
   effect  the  transfer,  must  properly  validate  and  verify   the
   correctness of the reported unexpended balance by the MBO to ensure
   that there would be no excess or insufficiency  of  fund  transfer.
   The unexpended balances of QRF and MF-MOOE at year end,  lodged  in
   the Special Trust Fund and specifically segregated into the year it
   was obtained, must be reported and communicated to the  LDRRMO  for
   the preparation of the Investment Plan. Further, it is advised that
   correcting entries be effected on Trust  Liabilities-DRRM  for  the
   amount of understatement in the account;


3. The LDRRMO must prepare LDRRMFIP annually composed of the plan  for
   the current year to be forwarded to the MBO and  for  the  previous
   years’ unexpended balance of QRF and MF-MOOE based  on  the  report
   received from the Accounting  Office.  The  LDRRMFIP  must  include
   under a separate caption,  the  list  of  projects  and  activities
   charged to the unexpended DRRMF  of  previous  years  after  proper
   evaluation of the need to use the Special Trust Fund. Also,  ensure
   that the 30 percent QRF is provided in the LDRRMFIP as  a  lump-sum
   appropriation to allow its immediate use in times of  calamity  and
   disaster; and


4. The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management  Office,  Municipal
   Budget Office and Accounting Office must reconcile their  respective
   records to determine  the  causes  of  discrepancies  and  make  the
   necessary adjustments and  to  establish  a  complete  and  reliable
   information on the appropriation, release and utilization of DRRMF.

We    recommended    that    the    Municipality    prioritize     the
correction/revision of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan upon  receipt
of the result of  Environmental  Management  Bureau  (EMB)’  evaluation  and
submit it to the EMB Regional Office V and NSWC for  approval,  as  required
under Section 16 of RA No. 9003 to ensure the efficient management of  solid
waste generated within its jurisdiction.

We recommended that Management direct the Solid Waste Management Board
to require the other 21 barangays of the Municipality to allocate a  certain
parcel of land through their Sanggunian for the establishment  of  MRF.  The
Management must also exert its best efforts towards full  implementation  of
relevant environmental laws.

We recommended that the Local School Board Council incorporate in  its
budget programs  and  activities  intended  for  Early  Childhood  Care  and
Development as required under Section 7 (b) of R.A. No. 10410.

We recommended that the Management  require  the  Chairperson  of  GAD
Focal Point System and  other  concerned  officials  to  implement  all  the
projects and activities programmed in the GAD Plan and Budget to ensure  the
optimum utilization of the GAD Funds for the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended to the  Management  to  require  the  Bids  and  Awards
Committee to prepare and submit  to  the  GPPB  the  procurement  monitoring
report within the time frame as stipulated in Section 12.2  of  the  Revised
IRR of RA 9184, so that monitoring and evaluation of the LGUs compliance  to
the provisions of the Procurement Act can be made.

We recommended that-


1. The existing articles and provisions of the CY2010 Revenue Code  be
   revisited with the end-in-view  of  considering  the  call  of  the
   present times towards the best  interest  of  the  public  and  the
   government;


2. Update the existing tax rates, fees and charges prescribed  in  the
   Revenue Code to be able to sustain  an  adequate,  appropriate  and
   relevant tax rates in relation  to  the  Municipality’s  fast-paced
   growth and development requirements; and


3. Include the ordinance enacted/amended from  CYs  2010  to  2019  in
   coming up with the revised Revenue Code.

We recommended that the LGU  thru  the  Municipal  Treasurer’s  Office
conduct inspection of the business establishments that are not  included  in
the database of the Municipality to actually validate the existence  of  the
business  establishments.  Require  the  immediate  imposition  of  business
taxes,  fees  and  charges  and  explanation  from   owners/proprietors   on
operating without the benefit of business permits.  When  necessary  and  in
accordance with the provision of the Revenue Code of  the  Municipality  and
the Local Government Code and its  IRR,  file  appropriate  charges  against
business  establishments  operating  without  the  benefit  of  permits   as
required.

We also suggested that the Municipal Treasurer  strictly  enforce  the
requirement of Section 152 of the LGC where barangay clearances  are  to  be
submitted by business establishments before  business  taxes  are  paid  and
permits are issued.

We recommended the following:


1. That the Municipality, through  the  Municipal  Treasurer  strictly
   follow the procedural guidelines prescribed in the Revenue Code and
   the IRR of  the  Local  Government  Code  for  the  termination  or
   retirement of business;


2. That the Municipality strictly implement the provision  of  Section
   171 of the Local Government Code to ensure the accuracy of business
   tax collected and penalize erring business taxpayers  by  requiring
   them to pay tax deficiencies  including  applicable  interests  and
   surcharges; and


3. Collect the taxes due from these business establishments, including
   any interest, surcharges or penalties for non-payment or  delay  in
   payment.

We recommended that the Treasury Office and all  its  collectors
strictly implement the provision of Section 13(2) of the Local Revenue  Code
by requiring CTC taxpayers to file a sworn  declaration  of  total  earnings
derived from business, compensation, exercise of profession and income  from
real property during the preceding year.  The sworn declaration must be  the
basis for assessing the amount of CTC tax.

We recommended the following-


1. The Treasury Office strictly enforce the provision of Section  172,
   Article 30 of Local Revenue Code particularly on the Special Permit
   and Regulatory  Fees  on  selling/serving  liquors  throughout  the
   Municipality by conducting  inspection  on  all  business  entities
   within its  jurisdiction  to  ensure  that  stores  and  commercial
   entities selling liquors are strictly  regulated.   The  inspection
   may be conducted by requesting the aide of barangay  officials  and
   law enforcement personnel;


2. Institute  administrative  and  criminal  sanctions  against  those
   commercial entities found violating the  provisions  of  the  Local
   Revenue Code; and


3. Increase the rate of Special Permit and Regulatory Fees on  selling
   liquors and other sin-products through an ordinance, to  discourage
   the sale, disposition and consumption thereof in  order  to  ensure
   the health, peace and comfort of the inhabitants.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasury Office:


1. Institute control procedures in the testing, sealing,  calibration,
   monitoring and collection of fees from the  usage  of  weights  and
   measures by developing a system of monitoring to ensure renewal  of
   licenses or permits on or before the anniversary dates and maintain
   adequate  records   for   inspection,   calibration   and   sealing
   activities;


2. Conduct periodic testing to provide assurance on the fitness of the
   weighing instruments for the protection  of  the  buying  populace.
   Inspection  activities  must  likewise  be  properly  planned   for
   scheduling of personnel and the area to be visited; and


3. Recommend to the Legislative the increase of levy  on  weights  and
   measures considering that the rate as provided in the Local Revenue
   Code is already outdated by the economic inflationary  factors  and
   market exchange values.

We  recommended  to  stop  the  practice  of  collecting  money   from
patients/clients of the Municipal  Health  Office  on  various  services  it
offers until there is a clear and valid legal basis to do the same. If,
indeed, these collections are authorized  and  legal,  the  Management  must
require an Accountable Officer, from the Office of the Municipal  Treasurer,
who must be responsible for collecting and  remitting  the  amount  for  the
account of the LGU. Moreover, those previous collections must  be  accounted
and refunded, immediately.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to deliberate properly on the appropriate mode of procurement to be used before the LGU procure the needed services giving paramount consideration on pursuing the best interest of the Municipality.

a.) It is also suggested that they evaluate their current system and existing manpower for any necessary improvement or augmentation for better internal control system and to prevent the occurrence of the same deficiencies in the succeeding financial years.

b.) We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to observe the proper account classification in recording transactions in consonance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 so as not to mislead users of the financial reports. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality cause the immediate closure of the LGU’s existing controlled dumpsite and construct a sanitary landfill. The Management must also exert its best efforts towards full implementation of relevant environmental laws. Further, we recommended that the Management prioritize the submission of the 10-year solid waste management plan to the National Solid Waste Management Commission for its approval, as required under Section 16, Article 1 of RA 9003 to ensure the efficient management of solid waste generated within its jurisdiction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCPC prepare an ideal plan whose programs/projects/activities are allowed in accordance with RA No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120, and fast track its implementation for social betterment and protection of all its children within the municipality. Urgent activities must be given utmost consideration such as the passing of Local Code for Children and other ordinances for the betterment and protection of children.

We recommended that:

b. Implement fully the identified projects under the 20% Development Fund; and 

c. The members of the BAC must schedule early all procurement activities pertaining to development projects to pave way for their timely implementation and completion.

observations.reason: Projects under the 20% DF were still not implemented on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office effect efficient contingency plan that address the root cause of vulnerabilities to disaster. Only those programs/projects/activities which are related to disaster risk prevention and mitigation must be included in the comprehensive plan and be timely implemented to prevent and mitigate the effect of unseen disaster.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to the Management:

Require the concerned Accountable Officers to restitute all collections by depositing immediately in full with the authorized government depository bank; Henceforth, require all incumbent collectors to remit their collections daily and the Liquidating Officer to deposit within the prescribed period to avoid incurrence of shortages and/or misuse of public funds; 

Require the Accounting Office to ensure regular reconciliation of balances and prepare subsidiary ledgers for all accountable officers to properly and correctly determine respective accountabilities; and 

Institute appropriate administrative and/or legal actions against the accountable officers for non-compliance with the above-cited regulations, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The undeposited collection of the incumbent Municipal Treasurer was already deposited in January 2019.
observations.reason: There is still undeposited collection by the former OIC-Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:

Demand immediate refund of the outstanding cash advances of all concerned accountable officers pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002; Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant shall strictly monitor the grant and liquidation of cash advances and stop granting additional CAs unless the previous cash advance is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made pursuant to Section 89 of PD No. 1445; and 

Institute appropriate legal action for the immediate settlement of long outstanding cash advance granted to officers and employees.
observations.managementAction: Most of the cash advances granted during the year were already liquidated.
observations.reason: Previous cash advances remained unliquidated for several years and the accountable officers to whom these are granted were not anymore connected with the LGU.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant: Apply accounting standards and method of recording supplies and materials consistently cognizant of the accrual basis of accounting required under PPSAS Nos. 1 and 12; and Coordinate with the General Services Office for the setting up of internal control measures that will enhance procurement and custody of inventories and accountability thereof. The Supply Officer/s should utilize the stock cards and reconcile the same with the accounting records as often as necessary.
observations.reason: Some inventory accounts remained non-moving for several years, casting doubt as to their existence and correctness of the balances presented. Moreover, no physical count was conducted for inventory items.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management: Require the General Services Officer to maintain updated Property Cards per category on all property, plant, and equipment as recorded in the list of Property, Plant, and Equipment of accounting records, and thereafter as the transaction occurs; Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain property ledger cards consistent with their accounting records; Require both offices to reconcile property records ensuring that the balances shown in the prepared RPCPPE agreed with the supply records and the book balances in the accounting records, and vice versa; For any resulting difference, the Accounting and the General Services Office should work out on tracing the reconciling items, establish its existence/non-existence and determine from there any further action that may be undertaken; and Maintain and renew all Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment every three years for the signature of the holder of each item listed in the prepared RPCPPE or based on the old Memorandum Receipts on file.
observations.managementAction: Property cards for existing and newly acquired assets were already prepared and maintained by the GSO.
observations.reason: The Accounting still failed to prepare and maintain property ledger cards. There is still no reconciliation made between property and accounting records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the continuous and sustained effort of officers concerned in their adherence to existing laws governing the withholding and remitting of Inter-Agency Payables within the regulatory period.

observations.reason: The consolidated balance of inter-agency payables as of Dec. 31, 2018 significantly amounts to ₱46.36 million, although it decreased from ₱72.33 million in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive: Direct all department heads involved in development projects to monitor implementation of proposed programs and projects and to ensure that funds allocated thereon are utilized for projects identified as priority socio-economic and environmental development in accordance with the guidelines/policies prescribed under DILG/DBM Joint Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011; Instruct the MPDO to include in the AIP, those that are proposed for implementation for a particular year, determined as priority projects and adequately funded in the approved budget. A monthly report on the status of the projects shall be submitted for enhanced monitoring and control; Likewise, instruct the Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the implementation of the developmental projects to ensure that completion is met on time as scheduled; and Require the Accountant to determine and enforce the liquidated damages on delayed projects’ completion.
observations.managementAction: The LGU, in formulating the CY 2018 proposed projects, abided with the JMC particularly the non-inclusi on of not allowable projects under the 20% DF.
observations.reason: Projects under the 20% DF were still not implemented on time.

observations.recommendation: In view thereof, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive restrain DepEd officials concerned from charging expenses that are not within the bounds of laws and regulations on SEF. This recommendation is without prejudice to the suspension or disallowance of the subject expenses that are not in accordance with RA No. 7160 and RA No. 5447.

We recommended that the Municipal Chief Executive, the Solid Waste Management Board, the Municipal Environmental and Natural Resources Office, the Municipal Planning and Development Office and all others concerned give their full attention and cooperation in the full and effective implementation of the provisions of RA No. 9003. People and other waste generators shall be educated properly on waste source management and proper disposition. Likewise, through ordinance and in pursuant to Section 47, RA 9003, garbage collection fees may be enforced and be included in the business permit fees of those business entities. Household garbage collection fees may be charged and collected on monthly basis through the barangays with co-equal share between latter and the Municipality. The funds collected shall be exclusively appropriated to solid waste management programs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the concerned personnel to strictly comply with the existing rules and regulations prescribing the guidelines in the formulation of Annual GAD Plan and Budget, development of agency GAD activities, implementation and monitoring of the GAD Plan and Budget and the preparation and submission of GAD Accomplishment Reports. Utilize the GAD Fund only for acceptable programs, projects and activities that would address the identified gender issues in the locality based on the Magna Carta for Women-RA 9710 and its IRR, PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and other relevant international commitments.

We recommended that Section 13 of IRR of RA No. 9184 be strictly observed to enhance the transparency of the procurement process and in order to promote the ideals of good governance of the state including the local government units. We suggested that the office of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) shall revisit the provisions regarding the appropriate Observers on specific type of programs/projects/activities to be bid out to enhance the procurement process of the agency. Moreover, the BAC Secretariat shall be well acquainted with the necessary documentary requirements for the auditorial and technical review of all contracts and Inspection of Government Projects per COA Memoranda Nos. 91-704 dated February 27, 1991 and 2005-027 dated February 28, 2005. Relative thereto, the agency shall be guided with the Checklists provided in the implementation of the future procurement transactions of the municipality in the ensuing years. Moreso, remind the Municipal Accountant to require the attachment of complete supporting documents on the duly processed disbursement vouchers pertaining to procurement transactions of the municipality, as prescribed in Section 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to avoid future suspension and/or disallowance in audit.

In addition, a monthly monitoring report of the Project Status should be prepared by the local officials concerned for verification by the COA Technical Audit Specialist.
